stories.title: Mesa West Capital Provides $83M for Seattle Apartment Community
stories.excerpt: Mesa West Capital has provided an $82.5 million first mortgage loan to a joint venture between Harrison Street Asset Management and Security Properties to refinance Olin Fields, a 352-unit garden-style apartment community in the Seattle suburb of Everett, Washington. The five-year, non-recourse financing was arranged by Jesse Weber, Scott Williams, and Kevin Coyle out of CBRE’s San Francisco office. Built on 16 acres, the 21-building property offers a mix of one-, two-, and three-bedroom units featuring washers and dryers, stainless steel appliances, digital thermostats, quartz countertops, and fireplaces. Since acquiring the property in 2022, the sponsor has invested significantly in exterior improvements and renovations to 76 units. A portion of the loan proceeds will be used to complete the remaining interior upgrades. “The Holly submarket has had no meaningful deliveries since 2014, with more than half of the existing inventory consisting of pre 1995 product,” said Josh Westerberg, Head of Mesa West Capital’s West Region, who led the origination team out of San Francisco along with JJ McMahon. The post Mesa West Capital Provides $83M for Seattle Apartment Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mesa West Capital provides $82.5M refi for 352-unit Everett, WA garden apartments
Holly submarket has zero meaningful supply since 2014; >50% inventory pre-1995
Sponsor reinvesting in unit and exterior upgrades; loan funds final interior work
stories.summary: Mesa West Capital closed an $82.5M first mortgage refinance for Olin Fields, a 352-unit garden-style community in Everett, Washington's Holly submarket. The five-year, non-recourse loan supports ongoing capital improvements to 76 units and exterior upgrades. The Holly submarket has experienced minimal new supply since 2014, with over half of existing inventory built before 1995.
stories.impact: The tight supply and aged product stock in Holly submarket (Seattle suburban) signals strong rental demand tailwinds and pricing power for well-maintained assets. This creates a favorable window for new construction or value-add acquisitions in this undersupplied suburban Seattle market before competitive supply arrives.

stories.title: Linlo Properties Acquires West Shore Office Center in Pennsylvania
stories.excerpt: Linlo Properties has acquired the seven-story, 91,306-square-foot office building located at 214 Senate Avenue in Camp Hill, Pennsylvania. Situated on 4.44 acres in the heart of the Camp Hill Business District, the property benefits from its prime location bordering Route 15 Bypass and adjacent to Penn State Holy Spirit Hospital. Currently, the West Shore Office Center boasts 100% occupancy, featuring a prestigious tenant roster that includes Morgan Stanley, Merrill Lynch, JEM Group, Hersha Hospitality, and the Army Corp. of Engineers. Purchasing this property adds a fully leased, Class A asset to Linlo’s growing portfolio of properties across Central Pennsylvania, all of which Linlo owns, builds, and manages with its own in-house team. The acquisition followed a marketing process, during which ownership and CBRE selected Linlo Properties to acquire the property. CBRE’s Steve Marzullo, Adam Silverman, Jeremy Shyk, and David Sickler represented the seller and directly procured the buyer. This deal marks the second consecutive sale of 214 Senate Avenue for the team, with the first occurring in 2017 as part of a larger $20.6 million, two-property sale. The post Linlo Properties Acquires West Shore Office Center in Pennsylvania appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Linlo Properties acquires 91K SF Class A office in Camp Hill, PA at 100% occupancy
Property features Morgan Stanley, Merrill Lynch, Army Corps of Engineers as tenants
Second consecutive sale of same asset in nine years; no multifamily relevance detected
stories.summary: Linlo Properties acquired the 91,306-square-foot West Shore Office Center in Camp Hill, Pennsylvania, a fully leased Class A office building with a blue-chip tenant roster including Morgan Stanley, Merrill Lynch, and the Army Corps of Engineers. The deal marks Linlo's continued build-and-hold strategy in Central Pennsylvania. This is a pure office transaction with minimal bearing on multifamily fundamentals.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. Pure office acquisition with no signal on residential demand, supply, or capital flows affecting apartment markets.

stories.title: Gowanus Wharf Developers Secure $785M Financing for Campus’ Fifth Building
stories.excerpt: Charney Companies, Tavros and Incoco Capital closed on the construction capitalization for 175 Third St., the fifth building in the Gowanus Wharf campus. Apollo and Affinius Capital LLC are providing $600 million in debt. RXR is providing $185 million in equity. When completed, 175 Third St. will be the largest building in Gowanus, with approximately 1.1 million square feet and nearly 1,100 apartments across 27 stories. Twenty-five percent of the development’s units will be permanently affordable. “Securing this financing with Apollo, Affinius and RXR validates both the strength of 175 Third Street and our long-term commitment to this waterfront community,” said Justin Pelsinger, partner and COO at Charney. “We are excited to begin construction on this transformative building alongside the canal, and we expect it, and our other properties at Gowanus Wharf, to enhance the dynamic of living and lifestyle in the neighborhood.” JLL senior managing directors Christopher Peck and Peter Rotchford and senior director Nicco Lupo arranged the financing. The post Gowanus Wharf Developers Secure $785M Financing for Campus’ Fifth Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Gowanus Wharf 175 Third St: 1.1M SF, 1,100 units, $785M financing (Apollo $600M debt, RXR $185M equity)
Largest building in Gowanus; 27 stories with 25% permanent affordability requirement
Construction to start soon; validates lender appetite for dense urban waterfront apartments
stories.summary: Charney Companies, Tavros, and Incoco Capital closed $785M in financing for 175 Third Street, the fifth and largest building at Gowanus Wharf in Brooklyn. Apollo and Affinius Capital provided $600M in debt, while RXR injected $185M in equity. The 27-story, 1.1M-SF project will deliver approximately 1,100 apartments with 25% permanently affordable units at completion in 2031.
stories.impact: Strong debt and equity appetite for dense, transit-oriented urban residential in waterfront markets signals continued capital availability for multifamily in NYC metros, despite higher rates. Gowanus's repeated successful closings (fourth Affinius deal) demonstrate lender confidence in supply-constrained urban submarkets with permanence. This validates development economics for high-rise, mixed-tenure builds in transit-rich Brooklyn neighborhoods.

stories.title: Suburban Office Leads as U.S. CRE Pricing Posts Best Annual Gain Since October 2022
stories.excerpt: U.S. commercial property prices rose in May, with the RCA CPPI US National All-Property Index posting its strongest annual gain since October 2022, MSCI Real Assets said Thursday. The index increased 1.6% from a year ago and rose 0.4% from April, implying a faster annualized pace of growth of 5.4%. Suburban office prices led all property types in May, rising 4.6% from a year earlier and gaining 0.7% from April, a monthly advance that, when annualized, puts the pace at 9.3%. CBD office prices rose 1.4% year-over-year and 0.1% from April. The index has posted 10 months in a row of positive annual growth, though it remains 50% below its March 2022 peak. Retail prices declined 1.1% Y-O-Yand apartment prices declined 1.5% YOY in May. The industrial index rose 0.5% from a year earlier in May, the ninth consecutive month of slowing annual growth. However, industrial prices remain the only major property sector to have posted positive returns since the onset of the interest rate shocks in mid-2022. By contrast, apartment and retail prices sit 20% and 7% below that same point, respectively The post Suburban Office Leads as U.S. CRE Pricing Posts Best Annual Gain Since October 2022 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: U.S. apartment prices declined 1.5% YoY in May; down 20% since mid-2022 rate shock
Suburban office leading CRE pricing (+4.6% YoY); industrial slowing but still positive
Index posts 10 consecutive months of positive annual growth; apartment sector lagging recovery
stories.summary: U.S. commercial property prices rose 1.6% year-over-year in May (strongest since October 2022), but apartment prices declined 1.5% YoY and sit 20% below mid-2022 levels. Suburban office led all sectors (+4.6% YoY), while CBD office, retail, and apartments lagged. Industrial remains the only major sector with positive returns since the 2022 rate shock, though growth has slowed nine consecutive months.
stories.impact: Apartment valuations remain under pressure nationwide despite broader CRE recovery. Compressed pricing creates acquisition opportunities for developers with floating-rate debt exposure or those able to lock long-term financing, but underwriting must reflect persistent cap-rate expansion and slower rent growth than pre-2022. Markets with supply constraints (Gowanus, Seattle Holly) will outperform generic markets in next cycle.

stories.title: Florida People & Companies, June 26, 2026
stories.excerpt: Illustrated Properties, with more than 50 years of industry leadership, launched a dedicated Commercial Real Estate Division. The new division is led by Director of Commercial Real Estate Shane McCain, a widely recognized figure in the South Florida commercial real estate community. The division brings a broad range of property types, including office, retail, industrial, multifamily, land, hospitality, and medical. Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of Miami Gardens Center, a 52,000 square foot two-building retail center in Miami-Dade County. The asset sold for $11.1 million. Kirk Olson and Drew Kristol of Marcus & Millichap exclusively marketed the 100% leased property on behalf of the seller, Freshwater Group, and procured the buyer, Golden Horizon Terryville Corp, out of Hallandale Beach, FL.   Miami Gardens Center is located at 4880 NW 183rd St. and was built in 2006. JLL added Andrew Frey as Managing Director for the South Florida team. Frey is the former Head of Real Estate for the City of Miami. Frey will focus on serving public-sector clients and private-sector clients doing business with the public sector, including government entities, transportation agencies, higher education institutions and healthcare providers, leveraging his extensive industry expertise and established relationships to support JLL’s continued growth across the region’s real estate landscape. Ocean Bank provided a $15 million loan for a new miniature golf and restaurant, PopStroke recreation center, under construction on International Drive. The borrower is JCS IDRIVE LLC. The center will feature three 18-hole miniature golf courses, restaurants, an outdoor gaming area, a kids’ playground and a pond on a 5.1-acre site at 8580 International Drive. The PopStroke restaurant will have 330 seats, while the Dune Dog restaurant will have an additional 175 seats. The center replaces the former Pirate’s Cove Adventure Park and will be the third PopStroke facility in the Orl
bullets: Florida roundup: Illustrated Properties launches CRE division; retail center sells; JLL hires ex-Miami CRE director
PopStroke recreation center on International Drive receives $15M construction loan from Ocean Bank
No direct multifamily supply, demand, or capital signals; South Florida market activity noted
stories.summary: Florida commercial real estate snapshot: Illustrated Properties launched a dedicated commercial division; Marcus & Millichap brokered the $11.1M sale of 100%-leased Miami Gardens retail center; JLL hired a former City of Miami real estate director for South Florida public/private sector work; Ocean Bank funded a $15M construction loan for a PopStroke recreation center in Davenport (Orlando suburb). No material multifamily news in snippet.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. Retail and hospitality activity in South Florida noted, but no signals on apartment supply, demand, financing availability, or cost pressures.

stories.title: Dallas secures $19M worth of incentives for potential Morgan Stanley move
stories.excerpt: Dallas City Council greenlit an $18.5 million incentive package Wednesday for Morgan Stanley’s potential Y’all Street move.  The financial services and investment bank will spend a little time in Downtown Dallas before moving to Uptown in 2031. The package coincides with Morgan Stanley earmarking $700 million for its new digs at 2401 McKinney Avenue, which will bring hundreds of employees to Dallas’s burgeoning financial hub.  Morgan Stanley has yet to formally communicate their plans, but documents filed with the city indicate the timeline, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Morgan Stanley can still opt out and head to Alpharetta, Georgia, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dallas secures $18.5M city incentives for Morgan Stanley HQ move; $700M spend by firm
Morgan Stanley plans 3,800 jobs in 700K SF Uptown campus by 2031; construction starts fall 2026
Firm temporarily occupies 255K SF downtown during construction; Trammell Crow building it for $650M
stories.summary: Dallas City Council approved an $18.5M incentive package for Morgan Stanley's planned move to a new $700M, 700,000-SF Uptown campus that will house ~3,800 employees by 2031. The bank will temporarily occupy 255,000 SF downtown before relocating. Trammell Crow is developing the Uptown building for $650M, bringing total project value to ~$1.3B. Construction begins fall 2026.
stories.impact: Major employer relocation to Dallas Uptown creates significant downstream multifamily demand. Inbound 3,800+ high-income financial-services jobs will drive renter demand across North Dallas, Uptown, and Plano markets, particularly for luxury and Class A apartments near the new Morgan Stanley campus and transit. This anchors long-term rent growth and justifies new supply in Uptown and surrounding submarkets through 2031+. Window to acquire land and start underwriting in Uptown/Uptown-adjacent markets now before competition intensifies.

stories.title: Skyscrapers encouraged: Development near transit in California’s biggest cities poised for major boost from new bill
stories.excerpt: Transit-oriented development in major cities across the Golden State is poised to take a much firmer shape with a piece of legislation that could help municipalities close the gap between housing demand and state-mandated planning goals. Assembly Bill 2074, dubbed the Downtown Revitalization Act, is quickly working its way through the state legislature. The California State Assembly passed the proposed law in a 55-5 vote on May 28 and the State Senate amended and referred it to the Senate Housing Committee last week. Authored by Assembly member Matt Haney of San Francisco, AB 2074 would supercharge housing development in seven […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: California AB 2074 mandates transit-oriented housing in 7 major metros; 150-min height, 25% 450+ ft buildings
Bill provides $500M low-interest revolving loan fund for developers to lower construction costs
Expedited approval for projects meeting labor standards; applies to LA, SF, SD, SJ, Sacramento, Oakland, LB
stories.summary: California Assembly Bill 2074 (Downtown Revitalization Act) passed the Assembly 55-5 on May 28 and targets housing density in seven major transit-rich metros: Los Angeles, San Francisco, San Diego, San Jose, Sacramento, Oakland, and Long Beach. Legislation mandates regional transit districts with minimum 150-foot heights and at least 25% of districts zoned for 450+ feet. The bill includes a $500M state-backed low-interest revolving loan fund and expedited entitlements for labor-compliant projects.
stories.impact: Transformative zoning enablement for multifamily developers in California's largest metros. AB 2074 removes height barriers, streamlines entitlements, and subsidizes construction financing—directly lowering development feasibility risk and cost of capital for high-rise apartment projects in LA, SF, SD, SJ, Sacramento, Oakland, and Long Beach. Developers should begin land assembly and pre-entitlement work immediately in these seven metros to capture zoning upside and $500M loan fund before applicant queue forms. Expect accelerated supply pipeline in 2027-2030.

stories.title: S3 Capital Lends $101M on Luxury Resort Project Near Orlando
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Urban Network Capital Group and Vertical Developments has landed $101 million of construction financing to build a master-planned luxury resort community in suburban Orlando, Commercial Observer has learned. S3 Capital provided the loan for the sponsorship’s multiphased Visions Orlando Resort & Spa project in Davenport, Fla. The 70-acre project when completed will comprise a mix of single-family homes, townhomes and condos along with resident amenities and retail. “Visions Orlando represents exactly the type of thoughtfully designed residential community we look to support,” Steven Jemal, managing director of origination at S3 Capital, said in a statement. “From a lender’s perspective, the presale-driven capitalization provides significant downside protection while the underlying tourism dynamics support long-term liquidity and buyer demand.” Located at 7007 Osceola Polk Line Road, the Visions Orlando Resort development has sold out all 460 residential units in Phases I, II, III and V, according to S3. Phase IV of the project features 277 condo-hotel units geared toward vacationers. The master-planned community is expected to expand through Phase VIII along with a 4-acre commercial component, according to S3. Community amenities will include a 10,000-square-foot clubhouse with a fitness center, pool, sports courts, wellness facilities, restaurant, bar, movie theater and golf simulator. Urban Network Capital Group and Vertical Developments did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached t acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: S3 Capital provides $101M construction financing for 70-acre Visions Orlando Resort & Spa in Davenport
Master-planned luxury resort: single-family, townhomes, condos, retail; 460 residential units (all pre-sold)
Presale-driven structure and Orlando tourism dynamics support lender appetite
stories.summary: S3 Capital provided $101M in construction financing to Urban Network Capital Group and Vertical Developments for Visions Orlando Resort & Spa, a 70-acre master-planned luxury community in Davenport, Florida (suburban Orlando). The project comprises single-family homes, townhomes, condos, resident amenities, and retail. All 460 residential units in Phase 1 are pre-sold, reducing execution risk.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to traditional multifamily development. Project is primarily for-sale residential (condos, townhomes, single-family) rather than rental apartments, with presale structure typical of resort-destination markets. Orlando market is absorbing both rental and for-sale supply; lender enthusiasm here reflects tourism-driven demand near Disney/theme parks, not primary residence multifamily rental fundamentals.

stories.title: Law Firm Alston & Bird Takes 170K SF at 51 West 52nd Street
stories.excerpt: International law firm Alston & Bird has signed a major deal to relocate its offices from Park Avenue. Alston & Bird, which provides legal counsel to Fortune 100 companies and private institutions, signed a 15-year, 169,664-square-foot lease at Harbor Group International’s 51 West 52nd Street, with plans to move its operations from 90 Park Avenue, according to landlord broker Newmark. Newmark did not disclose the asking rent for the building, also known as 51W52, but the average office asking rent in Midtown was $86.55 per square foot in May, according to a report from CBRE. “51W52 is now fully leased to a roster of prominent tenants, all of whom match the building’s prestige,” Jordan Slone, chairman and CEO of Harbor Group International, said in a statement. “The success of 51W52 validates our strong faith in the resilience of the New York office market, especially for the best trophy properties in the best locations.” Newmark’s Howard Hersch, Greg Conen, Scott Klau, Erik Harris, Jennifer Schreiber and Zach Weil negotiated on behalf of the landlord, while Brad Wolk, Matthew Barlow, David Goldstein, Joe Learner, Matthew Brainard and Laura Whelan of Savills represented the tenant. Savills did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Recent deals in the ominous 38-story tower include Kroll Bond Rating Agency taking 121,000 square feet in January and law firm Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe renewing its 144,312-square-foot lease in July 2024. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Alston & Bird law firm signs 170K SF, 15-year lease at 51 West 52nd Street, Midtown Manhattan
Firm relocates from Park Avenue; building now fully leased to prestige tenants; avg asking $86.55/SF in May
No multifamily relevance; office market signal for trophy Manhattan locations
stories.summary: International law firm Alston & Bird signed a 169,664-SF, 15-year lease at Harbor Group International's 51 West 52nd Street in Midtown Manhattan, relocating from 90 Park Avenue. The building is now fully leased. Midtown office asking rent averaged $86.55/SF in May.
stories.impact: No material relevance to multifamily development. Pure office leasing activity in Manhattan CBD. Does not signal residential demand, supply, or capital availability for apartments.

stories.title: Design Firm Décor Global Inks 7K-SF Lease at 231 West 39th Street
stories.excerpt: Manhattan’s Garment District has sewn up yet another design tenant. Décor Global, an apparel, accessory design and manufacturing company, signed a lease spanning 6,973 square feet at 231 West 39th Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The 12-story office building is owned by the LLC 231/249 West 39 Street Associates. Décor Global will use its new space for offices as well as a showroom for wholesale apparel sales, according to landlord broker and building manager Adams & Company. The length of the lease was not disclosed. The asking rent was $39 per square foot. “231 West 39th Street continues to attract companies that require space tailored to their unique business operations,” Adams & Company’s Jeff Buslik, who repped the landlord in the deal, said in a statement. “The building’s historic Midtown character, combined with its dense showroom presence and immediate access to major transit and neighborhood amenities, continues to make it a highly sought-after destination for fashion and design tenants.” Décor Global has offices all over the world, including in Los Angeles, Shanghai and Barcelona. The company’s website lists its current New York City location as 499 Seventh Avenue, just a five-minute walk from its new office. It is unclear when Décor Global will relocate. The tenant was represented by Michael Bolton of MBRE Advisors. Bolton did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Built in 1908, 231 West 39th Street is a 12-story office building between Seventh and Eighth Avenues. Other tenants at the property include apparel companies Evolutionary Apparel, Chance or Fate, and Korkhin Sales. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Décor Global design firm signs 7K SF lease at 231 West 39th Street in Manhattan's Garment District
Office/showroom use at $39/SF asking; building attracts fashion, design, and apparel tenants
No multifamily relevance; Manhattan office leasing in specialized submarket
stories.summary: Décor Global, an apparel and accessory design company, leased 6,973 SF at 231 West 39th Street in Manhattan's Garment District for offices and a wholesale showroom. The asking rent was $39/SF. The 12-story building continues to attract design and fashion tenants.
stories.impact: No relevance to multifamily development. Garment District office leasing activity; no signal on residential demand or housing supply.

stories.title: Commercial Real Estate Should Stop Overlooking Qualitative Data
stories.excerpt: Quantitative data drives and informs decision-making in the multitrillion-dollar commercial property sector, but what ultimately decides the outcome of an investment lies in the vastitude of qualitative data. The answers to a successful investment have never been limited to tangible, quantitative market or performance data, like rent or occupancy rates, assessed property values, comparable sales, or an asset’s projected return on investment or internal rate of return. Qualitative judgments can be elusive, but they dictate how a property is perceived and the fate of an investment. This data provides a lens into the past and present story of an area surrounding a property, and is invaluable to understanding how the life cycle of an investment may unfold. The stories told through this information can shape risk profiles and ultimately determine where capital is deployed and which markets get written off too quickly. Steven Song. Photo: Courtesy Diald AI. Artificial intelligence is transforming how commercial real estate can gather this information, and market participants would do well to not overlook it. In New York, a developer can change the name or address of a building to capture an easy win. When landlords rebranded the likes of 708 Third Ave to 10 Grand Central or 1250 Broadway to NoMad Tower, they changed how the market perceived these properties and captured considerably higher rents. Many smaller investors and developers across the country often don’t have the luxury of simply rebranding a building in such a way. In most cases, an investment is reliant on a bevy of qualitative factors that outline the inherent story behind a plot of land, a neighborhood or a submarket. Historically, finding and parsing such information has been arduous, if not inaccessible, and dependent on intuition, which adds to underwriting risk. It has also not been scalable. New technologies are changing that. Much of the data needed to confidently identify qualitative risk in a real es
bullets: Opinion piece on qualitative vs. quantitative data in CRE investment decision-making
AI tools can help gather qualitative data on neighborhood dynamics, risk, and lifecycle
No multifamily-specific news; methodological commentary
stories.summary: Thought piece arguing that commercial real estate investors over-rely on quantitative metrics (rent, occupancy, comp sales, IRR) and should better integrate qualitative data—neighborhood history, perception, lifecycle trajectory—into underwriting. Author notes AI can help surface qualitative insights faster.
stories.impact: No direct market impact. Commentary on investment methodology rather than specific markets, deals, or supply/demand/capital signals relevant to apartment developers.

stories.title: CareRite Centers Acquires Queens Nursing Home for $58M
stories.excerpt: CareRite Centers, a New Jersey-based physical therapy, elder care and rehabilitation provider, has acquired the Grand Rehabilitation and Nursing at Queens for $58.2 million, property records made public Wednesday show. The deal for the three-story rehab center building at 157-15 19th Avenue in Whitestone, Queens, and for an adjacent parking lot on 19th Avenue, closed April 28 and was recorded June 24. The seller was Clearview Land LLC, which appears tied to Grand Healthcare System CEO Jeremy Strauss, records show. Grand Healthcare is a New York-based network with about 12 skilled nursing and rehab centers. Mark Friedman, a principal and co-founder of CareRite Centers, signed the deal for the buyer. CareRite Centers did not immediately respond to a request for comment, while Grand Healthcare could not be reached for comment. The Grand Rehabilitation Center at Queens is a 179-bed facility that offers a variety of services including physical therapy, radiology and lab tests. The building has 47,000 square feet of built space, and 10,169 square feet of additional air rights, according to data from PincusCo. Market value for the property was designated by the city at $8.1 million in 2022. It’s unclear what CareRite, which operates three other facilities in Brooklyn and one in Manhattan, plans to do with its new Queens property. The Grand Healthcare system, which has an overall rating of 1.8 out of 3 stars from Medicare, was named in a lawsuit in 2024, which alleged the company committed fraud, and was ordered to pay a settlement of $21.3 million. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: CareRite Centers acquires Grand Rehabilitation and Nursing in Queens for $58.2M; senior care transaction
179-bed skilled nursing facility at 157-15 19th Avenue, Whitestone; closed April 28, recorded June 24
No multifamily relevance; senior care real estate acquisition
stories.summary: CareRite Centers, a New Jersey-based elder care and rehabilitation provider, acquired the Grand Rehabilitation and Nursing at Queens for $58.2M. The 179-bed skilled nursing facility spans 47,000 SF with adjacent parking lot at 157-15 19th Avenue in Whitestone, Queens. Transaction closed April 28 and was recorded June 24.
stories.impact: No relevance to multifamily rental apartment development. Pure senior healthcare real estate transaction. Does not affect apartment supply, demand, financing, or cost structures.

stories.title: Charney, Tavros snag $785M financing for historic Gowanus project
stories.excerpt: A joint venture led by Charney Companies and Tavros secured a new package of debt and equity for their historic Gowanus development. Charney, Tavros and Incoco Capital landed $785 million in financing for 175 Third Street, part of the larger Gowanus Wharf campus in Brooklyn, the Commercial Observer reported. It’s the fifth building in the broader vision for the neighborhood. Apollo and Affinius Capital issued $600 million in debt for the project, representing the fourth development in Gowanus Wharf involving the latter party. The former has financed development in the neighborhood previously. Additionally, Scott Rechler’s RXR injected $185 million in […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Charney/Tavros/Incoco close $785M (Apollo $600M debt, RXR $185M equity) for Gowanus 175 Third St
Duplicate of Article 3; fifth Gowanus Wharf building; 1.1M SF, 1,100 units, 27 stories
Lenders reinvesting after prior Gowanus successes; strong capital appetite for transit-dense urban resi
stories.summary: Charney, Tavros, and Incoco Capital closed $785M in financing for 175 Third Street, the fifth building in Gowanus Wharf. Apollo and Affinius provided $600M debt (Affinius's fourth Gowanus deal); RXR injected $185M equity. JLL's Christopher Peck, Peter Rotchford, and Nicco Lupo arranged debt. Prior Silver Point, Brodsky Organization, and Tikehau debt closed spring 2023.
stories.impact: Duplicate signal from Article 3. Reinforces strong capital availability for dense, transit-oriented urban multifamily in NYC waterfront markets. Repeat lender participation (Affinius, Apollo, RXR) validates development thesis for Brooklyn supply-constrained neighborhoods. Confirms multifamily investment thesis holds despite broader apartment price declines; trophy urban markets insulating against sector headwinds.

stories.title: Is DC the next hot luxury resi space? BSH panelists talk about navigating capital’s emerging high-end market
stories.excerpt: Long defined by easily-turned-over efficiency apartments, DC builders are working overtime to meet new demand for luxury housing in the nation’s capital. Last week, BSH and TRD brought representatives of the city’s design, development and operations communities together for a frank discussion about what’s working, and what isn’t, in the DC luxury space. The event was hosted at the BSH Experience & Design Center, the capital’s home for everything Bosch, Thermador and Gaggenau. Across the three speakers’ portfolios, one trend has emerged: today’s luxury buyers don’t crave fancy pools or double height lobbies. For them, convenient amenities, services and retailers […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: DC luxury multifamily panel discussion: EYA, District Wharf Properties share design/operations insights
Luxury renters prioritize lifestyle services (co-working, dining, retail) over amenities (pools, lobbies)
Wharf model: activate space pre-opening; create sense of place to drive move-ins
stories.summary: DC multifamily operators (EYA, District Wharf Properties) discuss emerging luxury market trends. Luxury tenants value time-saving amenities and services (co-working, private dining, retail access) over traditional luxury finishes (pools, double-height lobbies). District Wharf's strategy prioritizes pre-opening activations and neighborhood programming to establish place-making before residential delivery.
stories.impact: DC luxury rental market is maturing and signaling shift in tenant preferences from physical finishes to experiential services and lifestyle integration. For DC area developers building luxury apartments, differentiation lies in curated neighborhood programming, integrated retail/service amenities, and operational excellence—not design excess. Validates the Wharf's success model and informs positioning for competing new luxury projects in DC proper and suburban affluent corridors (Clarendon, Pentagon City, Bethesda).

stories.title: Swiss firm plans Breitling tower as swelling branded condo pipeline tests Miami’s limits
stories.excerpt: A Swiss investment and private equity firm plans a Breitling condo tower in Brickell, The Real Deal has learned, adding to a packed pipeline of branded residences that’s set to test how much Miami’s market can absorb.  South Florida is considered the branded capital of the Western Hemisphere and second in the world to Dubai. The tri-county region’s projects included established residential labels, as well as car brands and brands making their first residential foray ever such as Frida Kahlo Wynwood Residences.  Partners Group, in partnership with Empira Group, plans the $220 million B Residences that will rise 70 stories […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Partners Group/Empira plan B Residences (Breitling branded condo tower) in Brickell: 70 stories, 300+ units, $220M
Construction starts late 2028; completion 2031; private social club, rooftop pools, penthouses with private pools
Miami branded condo pipeline testing market capacity; Brickell Financial District location
stories.summary: Partners Group and Empira Group plan B Residences, a luxury Breitling-branded condo tower in Miami's Brickell Financial District. The $220M, 70-story project will include 300+ units, a 45,000-SF private B Social Club, rooftop pools, and penthouses with private pools. Designed by Arquitectonica, construction starts late 2028, completion targeted 2031.
stories.impact: Growing pipeline of branded luxury condos in Miami (including Breitling, Frida Kahlo, and others) tests absorption limits in Brickell. For multifamily developers, this signals: (1) sustained capital appetite for high-end residential in South Florida waterfront/financial districts; (2) competition from branded condo products may segment luxury market; (3) rental apartments still differentiated in price-point, flexibility, and operations. Developers should position Class A multifamily in Brickell at sub-luxury rents to capture renters priced out of branded condo purchase; Brickell rents likely to pressure upward as condo supply absorbs capital.

stories.title: Churchill seizing Pebb Capital’s Streeterville hotel eyed for apartments conversion in $28M foreclosure
stories.excerpt: Todd Rosenberg’s plan to convert a Streeterville hotel into apartments is ending in a $28 million foreclosure, capping a yearslong saga of financial distress and insider dealmaking for the property at 201 East Delaware Place. Rosenberg’s firm, Boca Raton, Florida-based Pebb Capital, last month agreed to surrender the 18-story building to lender Churchill Real Estate. That company has offices in New York and Charlotte, North Carolina, and has been active in Chicago’s distressed property debt market, according to Cook County Circuit Court records. The hotel, which is out of operation, was known as the Gale Chicago in recent years and […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Churchill Real Estate forecloses on Pebb Capital's Streeterville hotel (former Raffaello); $28M distress sale
Hotel conversion-to-apartments plan stalled by debt distress; Chicago lodging market still struggling post-pandemic
Churchwick Partners also converting Sonesta Extended Stay to apartments; weak lodging demand driving residential pivot
stories.summary: Churchill Real Estate is foreclosing on Pebb Capital's 18-story Streeterville hotel at 201 East Delaware Place for $28M. Pebb had acquired the property (formerly Raffaello Hotel, recently operating as Gale Chicago) from Maxwelle Real Estate with intentions to convert to apartments, but distressed debt derailed the plan. Other Chicago developers (Churchwick Partners) are attempting similar hotel-to-residential conversions amid weak lodging recovery.
stories.impact: Chicago's hotel market distress is driving involuntary conversions to apartments and complicating sponsor execution timelines. For Chicago multifamily developers, this signals: (1) weak lodging demand persists downtown, reducing viable conversion economics and extending timelines; (2) distressed debt on hotel assets is tightening lender willingness to finance conversion projects; (3) Streeterville remains supply-constrained and high-value for rental apartments, but conversion financing is challenging. Existing apartment developers have advantage over hotel-pivot plays; conversions face longer debt workouts and lower returns than ground-up or stabilized-asset plays.
